figures.gloss: The supreme, distant creator god. Known interchangeably as Olorum (Yoruba origins) or Zambi (Bantu origins), he is the ultimate, undivided source of cosmic energy from which all Orixás and spirit lines emanate.
figures.facet: Maintained as the absolute monotheistic apex to satisfy both Catholic and Kardecist sensibilities, he is completely inaccessible to direct human petition, delegating all earthly interaction to the Orixás and the working spirits.
figures.provenance: Diana Brown 'Umbanda: Religion and Politics in Urban Brazil' (1994).

figures.gloss: The highest Orixá, embodying pure peace, cosmic balance, and unconditional love. He rules the 'White Line' of Umbanda, commanding the highest, most refined vibratory frequency of creation.
figures.facet: Explicitly syncretized with Jesus Christ, Umbanda's Oxalá sheds the traditional Yoruba myths of earthly blunders or drunkenness, becoming a flawless, Kardecist avatar of moral perfection and spiritual evolution.
figures.provenance: Roger Bastide 'The African Religions of Brazil' (1978).

figures.gloss: The spirits of enslaved elderly Africans who endured the brutalities of the plantations. They manifest in mediums as hunched, pipe-smoking elders offering profound wisdom, herbal remedies, and endless patience.
figures.facet: A radical inversion of worldly power. Those crushed at the absolute bottom of Brazil's historical hierarchy are elevated to the tradition's most revered, spiritually evolved masters of charity and psychological healing.
figures.provenance: Arthur Ramos 'O Negro Brasileiro' (1934); Diana Brown (1994).

figures.gloss: Spirits of indigenous Brazilian warriors and hunters who act as fierce spiritual guides. Working primarily under the line of Oxóssi, they utilize herbs, tobacco smoke, and vigorous dances to aggressively cleanse negative energy.
figures.facet: They represent the vital, untamed force of the Brazilian land. Unlike traditional ancestor worship, these original owners of the soil return specifically to spiritually heal the descendants of a mixed, colonizing society.
figures.provenance: Renato Ortiz 'A Morte Branca do Feiticeiro Negro' (1978).

figures.gloss: Spirits of the streets, rogues, and outcasts operating in the astral shadows. Distinct from the pure Yoruba Orixá, these are human spirits ('eguns') serving as karmic enforcers, boundary keepers, and messengers of the crossroads.
figures.facet: Exu absorbs the syncretic stigma of the Christian Devil but redeems it: he does the dirty work of spiritual cleansing in the mud of human vice, acting as a pragmatic protector rather than a principle of pure evil.
figures.provenance: Stefania Capone 'Searching for Africa in Brazil' (2010).

figures.gloss: The female counterpart to Exu, encompassing the spirits of courtesans, witches, and fiercely independent women. She is the mistress of the crossroads, female sexuality, romantic disputes, and subversive power.
figures.facet: She actively dismantles patriarchal expectations of the submissive, chaste woman. Her boisterous laughter and unabashed sensuality offer marginalized women a divine mandate for bodily autonomy, agency, and vengeance.
figures.provenance: Kelly Hayes 'Holy Harlots: Femininity, Sexuality, and Black Magic in Brazil' (2011).

figures.gloss: The central ritual working of an Umbanda terreiro. Mediums dressed in white form a circle, singing specific rhythmic hymns ('pontos') to the beat of drums to summon the descent of the Orixás and spirit guides.
figures.facet: It functions as an open, egalitarian spiritual hospital. The absolute core tenet is 'caridade' (charity): profound magical and psychological counseling dispensed entirely for free to anyone seeking help.

figures.gloss: The celestial citadel or spiritual homeland where the elevated spirits—especially the Orixás, Caboclos, and Pretos Velhos—reside. It is the astral realm of light, harmony, and intense spiritual preparation.
figures.facet: Aruanda fuses the Afro-Brazilian memory of a lost African homeland (Luanda) with Kardecist concepts of evolved astral colonies, creating a utopian afterlife defined by continuous spiritual labor rather than static rest.
figures.provenance: Roger Bastide 'The African Religions of Brazil' (1978).

figures.gloss: A young medium in Niterói through whom, at a Kardecist séance on 15 November 1908, the spirit later known as the Caboclo das Sete Encruzilhadas manifested and announced a new religion — open to the pretos velhos and caboclos that orthodox Kardecism turned away as insufficiently evolved to be worked.
figures.facet: Umbanda's founding event is a doctrinal argument staged as a séance: the spirits Kardecism excluded speak for themselves through Zélio and simply overrule the gatekeeping of the religion that excluded them.
figures.provenance: Diana Brown, 'Umbanda: Religion and Politics in Urban Brazil' (1994); Umbanda's own founding narrative as preserved by the Federação Espírita de Umbanda.

figures.gloss: The spirit who manifested through Zélio de Moraes at the founding 1908 séance, took the name of the Seven Crossroads, and set Umbanda's founding rule: that every spirit — however lowly its earthly station — may incorporate and work in charity.
figures.facet: A named founder distinct from the generic caboclo class he belongs to: he alone is credited with an institutional act, chartering a religion, rather than an ongoing pastoral office.
figures.provenance: Diana Brown (1994); early Umbanda chroniclers' accounts of the 1908 founding séance.

figures.gloss: Queen of the sea and maternal Orixá of the waters, ruling her own line in Umbanda's sevenfold scheme; each New Year's Eve (Réveillon), crowds on Rio's and other Brazilian beaches launch small boats bearing flowers, perfume, and mirrors as offerings for her favor in the coming year.
figures.facet: Umbanda's most publicly visible rite: a household devotion scaled into a mass civic beach ritual broadcast on national television, blurring the line between minority religious practice and secular Brazilian identity.
figures.provenance: Diana Brown (1994); Renato Ortiz, 'A Morte Branca do Feiticeiro Negro' (1978).

figures.gloss: Warrior Orixá of iron, roads, and demanda (spiritual battle), commanding one of Umbanda's most heavily invoked working lines; in Rio de Janeiro he is syncretized with St. George, whose 23 April feast has become a major civic and religious event.
figures.facet: Iron and the sword stand in for force applied in the service of order: Ogum's line is the one Umbanda calls on for direct confrontation with obstacles rather than counsel or healing.
figures.provenance: Roger Bastide, 'The African Religions of Brazil' (1978); Diana Brown (1994).

figures.gloss: Hunter Orixá of the forests and of knowledge won through the hunt; the roster's Caboclos spirits are explicitly said to work 'under the line of Oxóssi,' whose patronage they carry.
figures.facet: The forest, not the crossroads or the sea, is his domain: Oxóssi supplies the theology of the wild interior that the Caboclo line's herb-and-tobacco cleansings enact.

figures.gloss: Orixá of thunder, stone, and justice, ruling Umbanda's line of law and retribution alongside Oxalá's line of peace; in Rio he is syncretized with St. Jerome.
figures.facet: Supplies the tradition's theology of consequence: where Oxalá's White Line offers unconditional peace, Xangô's line insists that wrongs must be judged and answered.

figures.gloss: The sevenfold division of spiritual vibration under which Umbanda classifies every Orixá and spirit people it works with — commonly enumerated to include the lines of Oxalá, Iemanjá, Oxóssi, Xangô, Ogum, the caboclos, and the pretos velhos, though the exact list and its ordering vary by terreiro and federation.
figures.facet: Gives Umbanda's famously improvisational, house-by-house practice a single shared skeleton: whatever a given terreiro calls its lines, it is still counting to seven.
figures.provenance: Diana Brown (1994); David Hess, 'Spirits and Scientists' (1991).

figures.gloss: Child spirits, sometimes paired as twins, who incorporate as playful, mischievous children, unblocking stalled spiritual work and bringing joy to a gira; their principal feast day, 27 September, coincides with the Catholic calendar's Cosme and Damião and is marked with sweets distributed to children.
figures.facet: The only working line defined by developmental status rather than ethnic or occupational history: their gift is precisely their incompleteness, a lightness the tradition's more burdened spirit lines cannot offer.
figures.provenance: Diana Brown (1994); Stefania Capone, 'Searching for Africa in Brazil' (2010).

figures.gloss: Sigils drawn in pemba (chalk) on the terreiro floor to identify, invoke, and anchor a specific spirit during a gira; each entity — Orixá, caboclo, preto velho, or Exu — has its own recognized sign, drawn by the medium or by the incorporated spirit itself.
figures.facet: Writing stands in for naming: the terreiro floor becomes a page on which spirits sign themselves into presence, a visual counterpart to the sung pontos that call them.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: Olorum is the absolute but distant source; Oxalá is his highest, most direct manifestation in the vibratory lines of Umbanda.
Intra-tradition: The male and female principles of the 'Left Path' (street spirits), ruling the crossroads and matters of mundane human desire together.
Intra-tradition: The two pillars of Umbanda charity. The Caboclos offer vigorous, active, martial cleansing; the Pretos Velhos offer passive, patient, deeply psychological endurance.
Intra-tradition: The Gira acts as the temporary terrestrial anchor, pulling the luminous energies of Aruanda down onto the physical plane to heal.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicitly and formally identified with Jesus Christ in Umbanda, reinterpreting the Yoruba deity as a Kardecist avatar of moral perfection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Derives its name and trickster/messenger function from the Yoruba Eshu, but is heavily recontextualized as a collective of human spirits policing karma.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, infinite, and inherently unknowable divine source from which all differentiated spiritual forces emanate.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme white-clothed deity associated with the sun, creation, peace, and ultimate cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The liminal, occasionally deceitful messenger who rules the crossroads, guides souls, and freely traverses the boundaries of the upper and lower worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A shadowed, often feared entity associated with the margins, tasked with guiding human souls through the dangerous spiritual underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fiercely independent queen of both divine desire and underworld power, weaponizing sexuality and refusing patriarchal subordination.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fierce, hot-tempered feminine force intimately tied to the margins of society, disease, and the explosive power of women's vengeance and healing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The embodiment of absolute compassion—highly evolved spiritual beings who defer their own ultimate rest to endlessly counsel and heal suffering humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The powerful, untamed master of the deep forest who possesses intimate knowledge of the land, protecting it while sharing ancient survival wisdom.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A rhythmic, communal, circular dance utilizing music and physical exhaustion to trigger spirit incorporation for the explicit purpose of healing the community.

GOLD SEAM: In Brazil, Oxalá is overlaid on Christ as Senhor do Bonfim, and Umbanda's altars run on Catholic saint imagery generally — the same saints-as-mask mechanism operating a generation deeper into synthesis.

the Pomba Gira figure-type is shared across the Umbanda/Quimbanda boundary, with devotees, iconography, and named entities crossing between the two closely related Afro-Brazilian traditions in practice.
Zélio was the medium through whom the Caboclo das Sete Encruzilhadas spoke and founded Umbanda at the November 1908 séance.
The founding caboclo is the named instance of the wider Caboclo spirit class the roster already holds generically.
Umbanda's own doctrine holds that the Caboclo spirits work specifically under Oxóssi's line.
Oxalá's White Line, already in the roster, is one of the seven divisions this schema organizes.
Every gira depends on pontos riscados to identify and anchor the spirits it calls down.
The Erês are one of the working spirit lines a gira calls down alongside caboclos, pretos velhos, and Exus.
The same Yoruba-derived Orixá of the sea, venerated under the same name and iconography in Candomblé and its Kardecist-inflected offshoot Umbanda.
The same warrior Orixá of iron worked in both traditions, with Umbanda adding the specific St. George syncretism.
The same thunder-and-justice Orixá across both traditions; Umbanda additionally overlays St. Jerome.
Ogum is worked in Rio under the mask of St. George, whose 23 April feast is a major civic event — the same saints-as-mask mechanism already carried by Oxalá/Christ.
Xangô is syncretized with St. Jerome in the Rio tradition, extending the same saint-overlay mechanism to the line of justice.
The identical chalk-sigil practice, shared by name and function across Umbanda and its left-hand offshoot Quimbanda.
the same Obatala/Oxala complex re-adapted again in Brazilian Umbanda as the Orixá of Peace.

Umbanda fuses the withdrawn Kongo creator with the Yoruba Olorun into a single double-named monotheist apex ('Olorum/Zambi') serving Catholic and Kardecist sensibilities — a deliberate modern merger the Kongo original does not itself contain.
Umbanda draws its supreme creator from the Ketu-lineage Olorun preserved in Candomblé, then additionally fuses him with the Bantu Nzambi — Candomblé's own houses do not make that further merger.

Umbanda takes the Candomblé Oxalá and strips his myth-cycle (including the drunken failures), remaking him as a flawless Kardecist Christ-avatar and functionally the supreme active deity — a deliberate theological rewrite, not simple transmission.

Same Afro-Brazilian spirit-class across sibling religions: Umbanda works Pomba Gira under the law of charity, bound to evolve; Quimbanda enthrones Maria Padilha as a sovereign sorceress answering to no evolutionary law.

dossier.essence: A Brazilian mediumistic religion that took shape in the early 20th century — its own tradition dating the founding to a 1908 event near Rio de Janeiro involving the medium Zelio Fernandino de Moraes — by synthesizing Kardecist Spiritism, Roman Catholicism, and Afro-Brazilian (Yoruba Orixa and Bantu) traditions with Indigenous elements. Widespread across Brazil and the Southern Cone, its defining act is spirit incorporation performed as free charity (caridade).
dossier.cosmos: A remote creator (Olorum, also called Zambi) presides over a hierarchy of Orixas — here largely aligned with Catholic saints and with Kardecist 'vibrational lines' — beneath whom work ranks of disembodied human spirits at differing stages of moral evolution: the wise Pretos Velhos (spirits of old enslaved Africans), the Caboclos (Indigenous warrior-spirits), child-spirits, and the liminal crossroads-spirits Exu and Pomba Gira. From Spiritism, souls reincarnate and evolve across many lives; the elevated spirits dwell in the luminous Aruanda, and suffering is karmic and remediable through charitable spirit-work.
dossier.practice: Worship centers on the gira, a session in a terreiro where white-clad, drummed mediums sing invocations (pontos) to incorporate the guiding spirits, who then give free consultations, healings, and 'passes.' Offerings, herbal baths, and the ritual use of tobacco and cachaca for certain spirits, together with the training of mediumship, structure the community; the cardinal ethic is caridade, selfless charity. Houses range along a spectrum from 'whiter' (more Kardecist and Catholic) to more Africanized practice.
dossier.canon: No single scripture: an oral and musical corpus of sung pontos and ritual practice, framed by Allan Kardec's Spiritist works (e.g. 'The Spirits' Book,' 1857) and codified in various 20th-century Umbandist manuals, with authority resting in the terreiro and its spirit-guides.
dossier.reflects: The inversion of social hierarchy in the sacred — a religion whose most venerated authorities are the spirits of the enslaved, the colonized, and the outcast, and whose worship is dispensed as free healing.
sources: Diana DeG. Brown, 'Umbanda: Religion and Politics in Urban Brazil' (1986)
Roger Bastide, 'The African Religions of Brazil' (1978)
Lindsay Hale, 'Hearing the Mermaid's Song: The Umbanda Religion in Rio de Janeiro' (2009)
